v.s: The Qing army destroyed the anti-Qing 'Thirteen Families of Kuidong' led by Li Laiheng. With this, large-scale resistance in southern China was basically ended. The Qing had completed unification of China except for Taiwan.

v.t: Great Plague of London, about 100,000 die
v.s: In the spring, bubonic plague broke out in London; by year's end, about 100,000 had died (one quarter of the city's population). The rich fled, and King Charles II moved his court to Oxford. The plague was the last great disaster before the Great Fire of London in 1666.

v.t: Newton discovers general binomial theorem and calculus in embryonic form
v.s: While at Cambridge, Newton derived the generalized binomial theorem and began developing his 'method of fluxions' (calculus). He made these breakthroughs during the plague years 1665-1666, but did not publish until 1704.

v.t: France establishes colony on Saint-Domingue (Haiti)
v.s: France created the 'West India Company' and formally colonized the island of Saint-Domingue (present-day Haiti). The colony quickly became the richest sugar producer in the Caribbean, relying heavily on African slave labor.

v.t: Great Fire of London destroys most of the city center
v.s: From September 2 to 6, a fire that started in a bakery on Pudding Lane spread across London, destroying about 13,200 houses and 87 churches (including old St Paul's Cathedral), but only six deaths were reported. After the fire, Christopher Wren directed the rebuilding, using brick and wider streets.

v.t: France and the Netherlands ally against England
v.s: France and the Netherlands signed an alliance to counter English competition. Louis XIV began expansionist policies, aiming to take the Spanish Netherlands, leading to the War of Devolution (1667-1668).

v.t: Kangxi Emperor marries Empress Heseri
v.s: The 12-year-old Kangxi Emperor married the granddaughter of regent Sonin, Heseri, marking his gradual liberation from the regents. Empress Heseri later gave birth to Crown Prince Yinreng.

v.t: War of Devolution: France invades the Spanish Netherlands
v.s: Louis XIV, claiming the 'right of devolution' through his wife Maria Theresa (Spanish princess), invaded the Spanish Netherlands (present-day Belgium). The French captured several cities but, under pressure from the Triple Alliance of England, the Netherlands, and Sweden, signed the Treaty of Aachen (Aix-la-Chapelle), gaining some territory.

v.t: John Milton publishes 'Paradise Lost'
v.s: English blind poet John Milton completed his epic 'Paradise Lost' in 12 books, recounting Adam and Eve's fall and Satan's rebellion against God. Drawn from the Bible, the poem also incorporates political allegories of the English Revolution; it is one of the most important epics in English literature.

v.t: Kangxi Emperor takes personal rule
v.s: On the seventh day of the seventh lunar month (August 25), the 14-year-old Kangxi Emperor formally began personal rule in the Hall of Supreme Harmony. However, regent Oboi still held power and refused to relinquish it. With the support of Grand Dowager Xiaozhuang, Kangxi trained a group of youthful bodyguards and prepared to eliminate Oboi.

v.t: Triple Alliance of England, the Netherlands, and Sweden formed to contain French expansion
v.s: England, the Netherlands, and Sweden formed the Triple Alliance, demanding that France end the War of Devolution. Louis XIV was forced to sign the Treaty of Aachen, returning some occupied territories. This enraged the French king and sowed the seeds for the later Franco-Dutch War.

v.t: Newton invents reflecting telescope
v.s: Newton built the first practical reflecting telescope, using a parabolic mirror instead of a lens to avoid chromatic aberration. He ground the primary mirror himself; the telescope is still preserved in the Royal Society.

v.t: Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth and Russia sign Truce of Andrusovo
v.s: Poland and Russia ended the war that had begun in 1654. Poland ceded Smolensk, Kyiv, and Left-bank Ukraine (east of the Dnieper) to Russia. Russia gained extensive territory, and Poland's power was greatly diminished.

v.t: Kangxi Emperor seizes Oboi, recovers imperial power
v.s: In the fifth lunar month, Kangxi summoned his young bodyguards to practice wrestling in the palace. When Oboi entered, they arrested him. The emperor then announced 30 charges against Oboi, imprisoned him, and executed his associates. Kangxi began to truly control the court, and Qing politics stabilized.

v.t: Republic of Venice cedes Crete to the Ottoman Empire
v.s: After the 24-year Cretan War (1645-1669), Venice was forced to sign a peace treaty, ceding the island of Crete to the Ottomans. Venice lost its most important stronghold in the eastern Mediterranean, and the Ottomans completed their control of the Aegean Sea.

v.t: Dutch painter Vermeer dies, leaving 'Girl with a Pearl Earring'
v.s: Johannes Vermeer, master of the Dutch Golden Age, died. His painting 'Girl with a Pearl Earring' became world-famous. He produced fewer than 40 works, characterized by exquisite light and quiet interior scenes.

v.t: Secret Treaty of Dover signed; England secretly promises to support France
v.s: In May, Charles II and Louis XIV signed the secret Treaty of Dover. England promised to help France against the Netherlands and to restore Catholicism in England (the king secretly converting), while France provided financial support. The treaty was the background to the Third Anglo-Dutch War.

v.t: Hudson's Bay Company established in North America
v.s: Charles II granted a charter to the Hudson's Bay Company, giving it a monopoly on the fur trade in Rupert's Land (most of present-day Canada). The company was a pioneer of English colonial expansion in northwestern North America and still exists as a department store.

v.t: Qing court issues 'Coastal Evacuation Order' to cut off anti-Qing supply lines
v.s: To cut off supplies to the Zheng regime, the Qing ordered coastal residents from Shandong to Guangdong to move 30 li (about 15 km) inland, creating a scorched-earth buffer. The evacuation caused great suffering but did weaken the Zheng economy.

v.t: Molière's 'The Bourgeois Gentleman' premieres
v.s: Molière's comédie-ballet 'Le Bourgeois Gentilhomme' premiered at the Château de Chambord, satirizing a wealthy commoner trying to rise into the nobility. Incorporating Turkish ceremonial dance, it is one of Molière's late masterpieces.

v.t: Holy Roman Empire and Ottoman Empire sign truce
v.s: In 1671, the Holy Roman Empire and the Ottoman Empire concluded a temporary armistice. The Ottomans were preparing for a major campaign against the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth and needed stability on their western border. The Holy Roman Empire wanted time to deal with internal conflicts. The truce allowed the Ottomans to concentrate their forces for the invasion of Ukraine (1672), but peace in Eastern Europe lasted only a few years. In 1683 the Ottomans again besieged Vienna.

v.t: Wu Sangui actively prepares for rebellion in Yunnan
v.s: Wu Sangui, Prince of Pingxi, trained troops, stockpiled grain, and even minted his own coins in Yunnan. His growing power alarmed the Qing court, setting the stage for the Revolt of the Three Feudatories in 1673.

v.t: Franco-Dutch War breaks out; Louis XIV invades the Netherlands
v.s: In April, France, allied with England and Cologne, attacked the Dutch Republic. French forces rapidly overran much of the Netherlands. The Dutch opened the dikes and flooded the land, stopping the French advance on Amsterdam. The Dutch navy under Tromp repeatedly defeated the combined Anglo-French fleet.

v.t: Newton submits paper on light and color to Royal Society
v.s: Newton's paper proposed a particle theory of light and demonstrated that white light is composed of different colors. His conclusions were harshly criticized by Hooke, causing Newton to withhold his optical works for many years.

v.t: Qing court abolishes land confiscation (quan di) to ease Manchu-Han tensions
v.s: The Kangxi Emperor ordered a halt to the practice of Manchu nobles seizing Han land (quan di), which had displaced many peasants. The move helped social stability and economic recovery.

v.t: Revolt of the Three Feudatories breaks out; Wu Sangui rebels
v.s: In the 11th lunar month, the Kangxi Emperor ordered the abolition of the three feudatories. Wu Sangui in Yunnan rose in rebellion, proclaiming himself 'Grand Marshal of the Supreme Command'. Soon Geng Jingzhong (Jingnan feudatory) and Shang Zhixin (Pingnan feudatory) joined. The revolt engulfed several southern provinces, and the Qing court was in serious danger.

v.t: Third Anglo-Dutch War begins
v.s: England, under the Treaty of Dover, joined France in declaring war on the Dutch Republic. English and Dutch navies fought fiercely at Sole Bay and elsewhere, but the English could not achieve a decisive victory. England withdrew from the war in 1674, making peace with the Netherlands.

v.t: Leibniz introduces modern calculus notation
v.s: German mathematician Gottfried Leibniz, independently of Newton, developed calculus and introduced the integral sign ∫ and the differential symbol d. His notation is still used today. His priority dispute with Newton lasted his entire life.

v.t: England signs Treaty of Westminster, withdraws from Franco-Dutch War
v.s: In February, England and the Netherlands made a separate peace, ending the Third Anglo-Dutch War. England returned to the status quo ante, while the Netherlands ceded New Netherland (New York) to England. England thereafter opposed the Netherlands no more and instead turned to containing France.

v.t: Geng Jingzhong and Shang Zhixin join Wu Sangui's rebellion
v.s: Geng Jingzhong in Fujian and Shang Zhixin in Guangdong successively rebelled against the Qing and joined Wu Sangui. Their combined forces once occupied Zhejiang, Jiangxi, and other provinces, reaching the peak of the feudatories' power. However, Geng later surrendered to the Qing due to a conflict with Zheng Jing.

v.t: Shivaji crowned Emperor of the Maratha Empire
v.s: Indian Maratha leader Shivaji was crowned 'Chhatrapati' (emperor) at Raigad Fort, founding the Maratha Empire. He employed guerrilla tactics to seize large territory from the Mughals and became a symbol of Hindu resistance to Islamic Mughal rule.

v.t: Royal Observatory established at Greenwich; prime meridian later defined
v.s: Charles II appointed John Flamsteed as the first 'Astronomer Royal' and founded an observatory at Greenwich, London, to solve the problem of measuring longitude at sea. The site later became the location of the prime meridian (1884).

v.t: King Philip's War: New England colonists vs. Native Americans
v.s: Wampanoag chief Metacomet (called 'King Philip') led a coalition of tribes in attacks on New England settlements. The war lasted until 1676, with heavy casualties on both sides. The Native Americans were ultimately defeated and lost much of their land.

v.t: Leeuwenhoek observes microorganisms for the first time
v.s: Dutch scientist Antonie van Leeuwenhoek, using a self-made single-lens microscope, observed rainwater and scrapings from his teeth and found 'microorganisms' (protozoa and bacteria). He reported his findings to the Royal Society, ushering in microbiology.

v.t: Qing recover Jiangxi and Zhejiang; tide turns in Three Feudatories War
v.s: In the 15th year of Kangxi (1676), the Qing army achieved major victories in Jiangxi and Zhejiang. Geng Jingzhong surrendered under Qing pressure, and Zheng Jing's forces were driven out of Fujian. The Revolt of the Three Feudatories entered a phase of Qing counteroffensive; Wu Sangui was trapped in Hunan and unable to advance north.

v.t: Denmark and Netherlands form alliance against Sweden
v.s: In 1676, Denmark-Norway and the Dutch Republic signed an alliance against Swedish dominance and expansion in the Baltic. That year, Denmark and Sweden fought fierce battles at Lund and elsewhere. The Scanian War (1675-1679) lasted several years.

v.t: Stepan Razin rebellion in Russia suppressed
v.s: The rebellion of Cossack leader Stepan Razin against serfdom and the tsarist autocracy was crushed. Razin was executed in Moscow. It was the largest peasant war in 17th-century Russia.

v.t: Baruch Spinoza dies; 'Ethics' published posthumously
v.s: Dutch philosopher Spinoza died in The Hague; his 'Ethics' was published that same year. The book, written in geometric style, argues that God and nature are one (pantheism), profoundly influencing later philosophy.

v.t: Peace congress opens at Nijmegen to end Franco-Dutch War
v.s: European powers gathered at Nijmegen to negotiate an end to the war. The resulting treaties (1678-1679) gave France Franche-Comté and several cities in the Spanish Netherlands, making France the big winner.

v.t: Qing recover Hunan; Wu Sangui retreats to Yunnan-Guizhou
v.s: In the 16th year of Kangxi (1677), Qing forces made major gains against Wu Sangui. Important cities in northern Hunan such as Yuezhou (now Yueyang) and Changsha were recovered. Wu Sangui was forced to abandon his Hunan base and retreated to Guizhou and Yunnan. The Qing pursued with a policy of divide and rule. Wu Sangui died of illness in 1678 after proclaiming himself emperor at Hengzhou; morale among the rebels collapsed, and the Three Feudatories War entered its final phase.

v.t: Wu Sangui declares himself emperor, then dies
v.s: In the 17th year of Kangxi (1678), Wu Sangui proclaimed himself emperor of the Great Zhou state at Hengzhou, Hunan, with the reign title Zhaowu. But he was already gravely ill and died in August. His grandson Wu Shifan succeeded and retreated to Yunnan. The rebellion quickly collapsed.

v.t: Treaties of Nijmegen end Franco-Dutch War
v.s: France concluded treaties with the Netherlands, Spain, and the Holy Roman Empire. France gained Franche-Comté (the Free County of Burgundy) and several cities in the Spanish Netherlands. Louis XIV became the dominant ruler in Europe.

v.t: John Bunyan publishes 'The Pilgrim's Progress'
v.s: English writer John Bunyan wrote 'The Pilgrim's Progress' while in prison; it is an allegory of a Christian's journey from the 'City of Destruction' to the 'Celestial City'. A classic of Puritan literature, it became the second most-bought book after the Bible for many years.

v.t: English Parliament passes Habeas Corpus Act
v.s: In May, the English Parliament passed the Habeas Corpus Act, stipulating that no person may be imprisoned without trial and that any detainee may demand to be released (the writ of habeas corpus). This became a cornerstone of the English constitution.

v.t: Qing capture Chengdu and Chongqing; Three Feudatories War nears end
v.s: In the 18th year of Kangxi (1679), the Qing army won decisive victories in Sichuan. Generals Yue Le and Tuhai led forces that captured Chongqing and Chengdu. After Wu Sangui's death, his grandson Wu Shifan retreated to Kunming. The Qing adopted a strategy of granting amnesty to those who surrendered. The southern and western fronts of the rebellion were basically pacified; the Qing began to concentrate on the last remaining rebel stronghold, Kunming.

v.t: Gottfried Leibniz invents binary system
v.s: Leibniz published a paper systematically explaining binary (using only 0 and 1) and noted its similarity to the hexagrams of the I Ching. Binary is the mathematical foundation of modern computing.

v.t: Pueblo Revolt: Native Americans drive out Spanish colonists
v.s: Pueblo Indian leader Popé led a massive uprising in New Mexico, killing about 400 Spanish colonists and occupying Santa Fe. The Spanish were forced to retreat and only reconquered the area 12 years later.

v.t: Qing recover Fujian and Guangdong; Zheng Jing retreats to Taiwan
v.s: In the 19th year of Kangxi (1680), the Qing army achieved a decisive victory off the coast of Fujian, capturing Xiamen and Jinmen held by Zheng Jing. Zheng Jing withdrew to Taiwan, and all coastal islands came under Qing control. The Qing then implemented the 'coastal evacuation' order, moving residents 30 li inland to cut off supplies to Zheng. Zheng Jing died in 1681, succeeded by his son Zheng Keshuang. In 1683, Shi Lang led a Qing fleet to take Taiwan.

v.t: Hall of Mirrors built at Palace of Versailles
v.s: Louis XIV ordered the construction of the Hall of Mirrors (Galerie des Glaces) at Versailles, decorated with 17 mirrored arches and ceiling paintings, symbolizing the glory of the French monarchy. The hall became the center of diplomacy and court festivities.

v.t: Kangxi Emperor quells Revolt of the Three Feudatories
v.s: In the 10th lunar month, Qing forces stormed Kunming. Wu Shifan committed suicide. After eight years, the Revolt of the Three Feudatories was completely suppressed. The Kangxi Emperor consolidated central power, eliminated the threat of regional warlords, and Qing rule became stable.

v.t: William Penn receives Pennsylvania charter
v.s: Charles II granted a large tract of land in North America to Quaker leader William Penn to settle a royal debt. Penn established the Pennsylvania colony, which practiced religious toleration and democratic self-government.

v.t: Great Fire of Edo (Tokyo)
v.s: In 1681 (Tenna era), the Great Fire of Edo (Tenna Fire) destroyed large areas of the city, including many daimyō mansions, samurai residences, and commercial districts. The shogunate subsequently strengthened fire prevention measures, promoting fire-resistant construction and urban redevelopment.

v.t: French court formally moves to Palace of Versailles
v.s: Louis XIV ordered the entire royal family and the main government agencies to move to Versailles, forcing the nobility to live near him and thereby weakening their local power. Versailles became the center of French politics and the model for European courts.

v.t: Peter the Great and his brother Ivan V co-rule; Sofia regent
v.s: Peter I (Peter the Great) and his half-brother Ivan V jointly ascended as tsars, with their sister Sophia as regent. In his youth, Peter formed the 'play regiments' that later became the basis for his reforms.

v.t: Kangxi Emperor begins first eastern inspection tour, examines northeastern border
v.s: In the 21st year of Kangxi (1682), the Kangxi Emperor made his first eastern inspection tour. He visited Mukden (Shenyang) to pay homage to the ancestors and inspected Jilin Ula (present-day Jilin City) to assess border defenses. This reconnaissance prepared him for the later Sino-Russian conflict at Albazin.

v.t: Qing forces take Taiwan; Zheng Keshuang surrenders
v.s: In the seventh lunar month, Shi Lang's Qing naval forces captured Penghu. Zheng Keshuang (Koxinga's grandson) submitted and surrendered. The Qing incorporated Taiwan into its territory, establishing Taiwan Prefecture under Fujian Province. The Zheng regime era in Taiwan came to an end.

v.t: Battle of Vienna; Ottoman Empire suffers crushing defeat
v.s: On September 12, King John III Sobieski of Poland led a European relief force that defeated the Ottoman army besieging Vienna. The battle halted Ottoman expansion into central Europe and marked the beginning of Ottoman decline.

v.t: Kangxi Emperor holds first special examination (Boxue Hongci) to win over Han scholars
v.s: In the 22nd year of Kangxi (1683), to win the loyalty of Han Chinese literati, the Kangxi Emperor held the Boxue Hongci examination in the capital. More than 150 scholars recommended from various provinces took the exam; 50, including Peng Sunyi, passed and were appointed to the Hanlin Academy. This greatly reduced Manchu-Han tensions.

v.t: Qing lift maritime ban, open Guangzhou and other trading ports
v.s: The Kangxi Emperor abolished the coastal evacuation order, allowing coastal residents to return to their livelihoods, and opened four ports for foreign trade: Guangzhou, Xiamen, Ningbo, and Yuntaishan. Normal foreign trade with China resumed.

v.t: France and Holy Roman Empire sign Truce of Regensburg
v.s: France and the Habsburgs temporarily ceased hostilities; France consolidated its gains in Alsace and Lorraine. Louis XIV continued his 'reunion' policy to expand eastward.

v.t: Kangxi Emperor's first southern tour to inspect Yellow River works
v.s: The Kangxi Emperor toured south to Shandong and Jiangnan, inspecting the Yellow River, Huai River, and Grand Canal projects, and also visited the Confucius Temple to win over Han scholar-officials. The southern tours became a regular method of governance for Kangxi.

v.t: Louis XIV issues Edict of Fontainebleau, revoking Edict of Nantes
v.s: On October 18, Louis XIV signed the Edict of Fontainebleau, declaring Protestantism illegal, destroying Huguenot churches, and forcing Huguenots to convert to Catholicism. About 200,000 Protestants fled to England, the Netherlands, Prussia, and elsewhere, causing a loss of skilled manpower and economic damage.

v.t: Sino-Russian border conflict at Albazin begins
v.s: Qing general Peng Chun led forces to besiege the Russian outpost at Albazin (near present-day Mohe, Heilongjiang). Russian commander Aleksei Tolbuzin surrendered but later returned in violation of the agreement. The Qing besieged again, inflicting heavy Russian casualties.

v.t: Johann Sebastian Bach and George Frideric Handel born
v.s: On March 21 (or 31), Bach was born in Eisenach, Germany; on February 23, Handel was born in Halle, Germany. Both are towering figures of Baroque music; though born in the same year, they never met.

v.t: League of Augsburg formed to oppose French expansion
v.s: The Holy Roman Empire, Spain, Sweden, Bavaria, and others formed the League of Augsburg (also known as the Grand Alliance) to resist Louis XIV's territorial claims. This alliance formed the anti-French coalition in the Nine Years' War (1688-1697).

v.t: Second siege of Albazin; Russian forces surrender
v.s: Qing general Sabusu again besieged Albazin. Tolbuzin was killed by a cannonball. The remaining Russians surrendered. The Qing did not completely destroy the fort, leaving room for later negotiations.

v.t: First edition of Newton's 'Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica' published
v.s: On July 5, Newton's 'Principia' (in Latin) was published with funding from Edmond Halley. It set out the law of universal gravitation and the three laws of motion, laying the foundation for classical physics.

v.t: Newton's 'Principia' officially published
v.s: Newton's great work was formally issued (though the title page bears 1687). It is considered the greatest scientific book ever written, making physics an exact science.

v.t: Venetian shelling destroys Parthenon in Athens
v.s: During a war with the Ottoman Empire, Venetian forces bombarded the Acropolis in Athens. A shell hit the Parthenon, which was being used by the Ottomans to store gunpowder, causing an explosion that destroyed the center of the temple. This was one of the worst destructions of a classical building.

v.t: Qing establishes Taiwan Prefecture, formally ruling Taiwan
v.s: The Qing set up one prefecture and three counties (Taiwan Prefecture, Taiwan County, Fengshan County, Zhuluo County) in Taiwan, all under Fujian Province. The first prefect was Jiang Yuying. The early Qing adopted a passive development policy, restricting immigration from the mainland.

v.t: Glorious Revolution: James II deposed in England
v.s: In November, the English Parliament invited William III of Orange (stadtholder of the Netherlands) and his wife Mary (daughter of James II) to take the English throne. James II fled to France, and William and Mary became joint monarchs. The Glorious Revolution established parliamentary supremacy over the crown.

v.t: Nine Years' War (War of the Grand Alliance) breaks out
v.s: Louis XIV of France, taking advantage of the Holy Roman Empire's war with the Ottomans, invaded the Rhineland, triggering the War of the Grand Alliance. France fought the League of Augsburg (England, the Netherlands, Spain, Holy Roman Empire) on multiple fronts.

v.t: Kangxi Emperor's third southern tour, inspecting water conservancy
v.s: In the 28th year of Kangxi (1688), the Kangxi Emperor undertook his third southern inspection tour. He focused on the Qingkou waterworks at the confluence of the Yellow and Huai rivers, personally climbing the dikes to measure water levels. He also inspected the Gaojiayan Dam on Lake Hongze. He met with Jiangnan scholars and gentry, and summoned the accompanying French Jesuits to learn about European astronomy and calendar science. The tour demonstrated the emperor's attention to water management.

v.t: English Bill of Rights establishes constitutional monarchy
v.s: On December 16, Parliament passed the Bill of Rights, stipulating that the king may not suspend laws, levy taxes, or maintain a standing army without parliamentary consent, and guaranteeing free elections and parliamentary privilege. It established constitutional monarchy in England.

v.t: Sino-Russian Treaty of Nerchinsk
v.s: On September 7, the Qing and Russian governments concluded the Treaty of Nerchinsk, fixing the eastern border along the Argun River, Gorbitsa River, and Stanovoy Range. It was the first equal treaty between China and a foreign power and maintained peace along the northeastern frontier.

v.t: John Locke publishes 'Two Treatises of Government'
v.s: Locke completed the 'Two Treatises' while in exile in the Netherlands, critiquing the divine right of kings and proposing social contract theory and the right of the people to resist tyranny. It became a classic of liberal political philosophy.

v.t: Battle of Ulan Butung; Qing defeat Galdan
v.s: Galdan, khan of the Dzungar Khanate, invaded eastward. The Kangxi Emperor personally led a campaign and defeated Galdan's forces at Ulan Butung (present-day Hexigten Banner, Inner Mongolia). Galdan used a 'camel fort' tactic but was still routed by Qing artillery.

v.t: Battle of the Boyne: William III defeats James II
v.s: On July 12, King William III of England defeated the Franco-Irish army of former king James II at the River Boyne in Ireland. The victory secured William and Mary's throne and established Protestant ascendancy in Ireland.

v.t: Fort William built in Bombay; English East India Company strengthens base
v.s: The English East India Company built Fort William in Bombay (Mumbai) as a hub for trade with the West Indies. Bombay gradually replaced Surat as the company's main port in India.

v.t: Duolun Assembly: Kangxi Emperor incorporates Khalkha Mongols
v.s: In the fifth lunar month, the Kangxi Emperor met with the chieftains of the Khalkha Mongols (Outer Mongolia) at Duolun Nor (present-day Duolun, Inner Mongolia), mediating their internal disputes and formally incorporating them into the Qing realm. The Khalkha were organized into banners with appointed zasaks (hereditary heads).

v.t: Treaty of Limerick ends Williamite War in Ireland
v.s: In October, William III signed a treaty with the remaining Irish Catholic forces, allowing Catholics to retain limited rights, but the terms were broken by the Irish Parliament, leading to long-term Catholic oppression in Ireland.

v.t: French mathematician Rolle states Rolle's theorem
v.s: In 1691, French mathematician Michel Rolle, in his work 'Traité d'Algèbre', stated a theorem concerning the nature of roots of polynomial equations (Rolle's theorem). It is a foundation for the mean value theorem in calculus, though its importance was not fully appreciated at the time.

v.t: Salem witch trials begin
v.s: In Salem, Massachusetts, several girls claimed to have been bewitched, triggering a massive witch-hunt. By May of the following year, about 200 people had been accused, and 20 were executed. The case became a classic example of judicial injustice and mass hysteria.

v.t: French navy defeated by Anglo-Dutch fleet at La Hogue
v.s: On May 29, an Anglo-Dutch fleet defeated the French fleet at La Hogue (Cotentin Peninsula, France), preventing the planned French invasion of England. After that, France abandoned major fleet engagements and turned to commerce raiding.

v.t: Kangxi Emperor approves compilation of 'Collected Compendium of Mathematical Principles'
v.s: The Kangxi Emperor ordered the compilation of the 'Lüli Yuanyuan' (Source of Musical Tuning and Calendar Mathematics) series, which included the 'Collected Compendium of Mathematical Principles', an encyclopedia of mathematics that incorporated Western mathematical knowledge and promoted Chinese mathematics.

v.t: Great famine in Bengal kills about 300,000
v.s: Drought, compounded by grain hoarding by British East India Company merchants, caused a severe famine in Bengal, with an estimated 300,000 dead. This deepened local resentment against British colonizers.

v.t: French establish outpost on Lake Pontchartrain (precursor to New Orleans)
v.s: French explorer Pierre Le Moyne d'Iberville established a settlement on the Mississippi River, later to become New Orleans. The settlement became the capital of French Louisiana.

v.t: Kangxi Emperor restores provincial examinations (xiangshi) to win over Han literati
v.s: In the 32nd year of Kangxi (1693), the Kangxi Emperor ordered the resumption of provincial examinations across the country. Because of the Three Feudatories War and the Taiwan campaign, the civil service exams had been interrupted in some provinces. Restoring them brought many Han Chinese literati into the bureaucracy, effectively easing Manchu-Han tensions. Earlier (1679), Kangxi had also held a special Boxue Hongci examination to recruit reclusive scholars. These cultural policies consolidated Qing rule over Han Chinese regions.

v.t: Bank of England founded
v.s: On July 27, the English Parliament approved the establishment of the Bank of England to raise funds for the war effort. It became the progenitor of the British central bank, managing the national debt and issuing currency.

v.t: Queen Mary II dies of smallpox; William III rules alone
v.s: On December 28, Queen Mary II of England died of smallpox; her husband William III continued to rule alone. Mary's death meant that the succession would pass to her sister Anne.

v.t: Kangxi Emperor orders compilation of 'Kangxi Dictionary'
v.s: In the 33rd year of Kangxi (1694), the Kangxi Emperor ordered Grand Academician Zhang Yushu, Hanlin scholar Chen Tingjing, and others to compile a large dictionary of Chinese characters. Completed after six years, it contained 47,035 characters, following the format of the earlier 'Zihui' and 'Zhengzitong', and became the standard Qing-era dictionary.

v.t: Bank of England established: origin of modern central banking
v.s: In 1694, Scottish merchant William Paterson obtained a royal charter to found the Bank of England in London. The bank lent £1.2 million to the government to support the war with France, and in return was authorized to issue banknotes. The Bank of England was the first true central bank, responsible for managing the national debt, issuing currency, and eventually supervising commercial banks. Its successful model was copied worldwide.

v.t: Peter the Great launches Azov campaign
v.s: Tsar Peter I led a campaign against the Ottoman fortress of Azov to gain access to the Sea of Azov. The first expedition failed due to lack of a navy, but the second campaign (1696) succeeded.

v.t: France enforces Loire River customs duties under mercantilist policy
v.s: In the 1690s, the mercantilist policies of French Finance Minister Colbert were fully implemented. The government established toll stations along the Loire Valley to protect domestic manufacturing and restrict imports, while also setting up royal workshops and overseas trading companies to promote exports. However, Louis XIV's continuous wars drained the treasury, and mercantilist development faced serious financial difficulties.

v.t: Late-Ming thinker Huang Zongxi dies
v.s: Huang Zongxi, a late-Ming/early-Qing thinker and historian, wrote 'Waiting for the Dawn: A Plan for the Prince' (Mingyi Daifang Lu), criticizing absolute monarchy and arguing that 'the world is the foundation, the ruler is the guest.' He refused to serve the Qing and lived in seclusion as a writer.

v.t: Kangxi Emperor personally campaigns against Galdan; victory at Zhaomoduo
v.s: The Kangxi Emperor made a second personal campaign against the Dzungars, defeating Galdan at Zhaomoduo (near present-day Ulaanbaatar, Mongolia). Galdan fled with only a few dozen horsemen and was defeated and killed the following year. After this battle, the Qing controlled the Outer Mongolia region.

v.t: Jonathan's Coffee House (precursor to London Stock Exchange) begins operations
v.s: In 1696, stockbrokers in London's financial district began gathering at Jonathan's Coffee House in Change Alley to trade securities. Brokers there bought and sold government bonds and shares in the East India Company and other joint-stock companies. In 1773, these brokers formally established the London Stock Exchange. The trading at Jonathan's is considered the precursor of the modern securities market.

v.t: Peter the Great's 'Grand Embassy' to Western Europe
v.s: Peter the Great, traveling incognito, accompanied a grand embassy to the Netherlands, England, and elsewhere to learn shipbuilding and science, preparing for his westernizing reforms. His visit shocked Europe.

v.t: Treaty of Ryswick signed; Nine Years' War ends
v.s: On September 20, France and the Grand Alliance signed the Treaty of Ryswick. France returned most of its conquests but retained Strasbourg. Louis XIV abandoned his support for the Jacobite cause.

v.t: Charles XII of Sweden ascends the throne at age 15
v.s: On April 5, Charles XI died, and his 15-year-old son Charles XII became king. Neighboring Denmark, Poland, and Russia took advantage to form an anti-Swedish coalition, leading to the Great Northern War (1700).

v.t: Galdan commits suicide; Dzungar Khanate temporarily submits to Qing
v.s: The Kangxi Emperor made a third personal campaign. Abandoned by his followers, Galdan committed suicide near Kobdo. The new Dzungar khan, Tsewang Rabtan, submitted to the Qing, although he would later rebel again.

v.t: Streltsy rebellion in Russia; Peter the Great brutally suppresses it
v.s: While Peter the Great was abroad on his Grand Embassy, the Moscow streltsy (old-style musketeers) rebelled in support of Princess Sophia. Peter returned and executed about 1,200 rebels, and forced Sophia into a nunnery.

v.t: Thomas Savery invents steam pump for mine drainage
v.s: English engineer Thomas Savery invented the 'Miner's Friend' – a single-cylinder steam pump for draining mines. It was the first practical steam engine, though inefficient, and started the Industrial Revolution.

v.t: Kangxi Emperor's fourth southern tour, inspecting canal works
v.s: In the 37th year of Kangxi (1698), the Kangxi Emperor undertook his fourth southern inspection tour. He focused on the Huai-Yang Canal and the Yellow River junction, reviewing the river control plans of officials including River Governor Yu Chenglong. The emperor also received French Jesuit missionaries such as Joachim Bouvet, learning about European astronomy and cartographic techniques. The tour promoted water conservancy and Sino-Western scientific exchange.

v.t: Treaty of Karlowitz: Ottoman cedes Hungary
v.s: On January 26, the Ottoman Empire and the Holy League (Austria, Poland, Venice, Russia) signed the Treaty of Karlowitz, the first time the Ottomans ceded large territories (Hungary, Transylvania, etc.), marking the end of Ottoman dominance in central Europe.

v.t: France and England reach agreement to partition the Spanish Empire
v.s: France and England secretly concluded the Treaty of The Hague (First Partition Treaty) to divide the Spanish territories after King Charles II's death, causing dissatisfaction among other powers.

v.t: Kangxi Emperor's fifth southern tour: emphasizes 'Manchu-Han unity'
v.s: In the 38th year of Kangxi (1699), the Kangxi Emperor made his fifth southern inspection tour. He visited Shandong, Jiangnan, and Zhejiang, inspecting river works and receiving officials and gentry. During the tour, Kangxi stressed the policy of Manchu-Han unity, forbidding Manchu nobles from bullying Han Chinese. He personally went to the Ming tombs in Nanjing to pay respects to the Hongwu Emperor, winning over Jiangnan literati. The accompanying French Jesuit Bouvet and others conducted geographical surveys.

v.t: Great Northern War breaks out: Russia, Denmark, Poland attack Sweden
v.s: In February, Saxony (Polish King Augustus II) attacked Riga, then Denmark and Russia joined, aiming to partition Sweden's Baltic territories. Swedish King Charles XII rapidly counterattacked, defeating Denmark and Russia. The war lasted until 1721.

v.t: King Charles II of Spain dies; will leaves throne to French Duke of Anjou
v.s: On November 1, the childless King Charles II of Spain died. His will named Philip, Duke of Anjou (grandson of Louis XIV of France), as his successor, violating the partition agreements among European powers and leading to the War of Spanish Succession (1701-1714).

v.t: Qing suppresses Dajianlu rebellion, gains control of Kham region
v.s: In the 39th year of Kangxi (1700), Sichuan General Yue Shenglong led Qing forces to suppress a chieftain rebellion at Dajianlu (present-day Kangding, Sichuan). The Qing advanced west, gaining control over the Tibetan areas of Sichuan, laying the foundation for later delimitation of the Sichuan-Tibet border and administration of Kham.

v.t: Qing suppresses Galdan's remnants
v.s: The Kangxi Emperor personally led a third campaign against the Dzungars, defeating Galdan at Zhaomoduo (near present-day Ulaanbaatar, Mongolia). Galdan, abandoned by his followers, soon died of illness. The Kangxi Emperor accepted the submission of the Khalkha Mongol tribes and organized the Outer Mongolian region into banners, incorporating it into the Qing realm.
